stories.title: Michael Stern’s JDS to bring on partner Jeff Soffer at Mercedes-Benz Miami project, working on $1B loan
stories.excerpt: Michael Stern’s JDS Development Group is nearing a deal to bring on Jeff Soffer’s Fontainebleau Development as a partner and secure an over $1 billion loan for the Mercedes-Benz Miami condo project, The Real Deal has learned.  The deal, which has not yet closed, will replenish the project’s capital stack and would resolve litigation with the existing lender, allowing the nearly 800-unit branded condo project to resume. Byron Trott and Michael Dell’s firm, BD&T & MSD, is one of the lenders, according to sources.  The $1.06 billion financing package includes a C-PACE (Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy) component, which encourages […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: JDS Development secures $1.06B refinance with Fontainebleau Development partnership for Miami Mercedes-Benz condo tower
791-unit luxury condo project valued $2B+ at completion; resolves foreclosure litigation with prior lender Cottonwood
BD&T & MSD (Byron Trott/Michael Dell) among lenders; C-PACE component funds sustainable design features
stories.summary: JDS Development is finalizing a $1.06 billion construction loan and partnering with Jeff Soffer's Fontainebleau Development to unlock the stalled Mercedes-Benz Miami condo project, a 791-unit luxury tower valued above $2 billion upon completion. The financing package, led by Santander-type institutional and family office capital (BD&T & MSD), includes a C-PACE sustainability component and resolves months of foreclosure litigation with prior lender Cottonwood Management. The deal replenishes capital to resume development on one of Miami's premier mixed-use projects.
stories.impact: Signals capital availability returning to Miami luxury condo development despite prior market stress; demonstrates institutional investors (Byron Trott/Michael Dell) remain confident in high-end residential fundamentals in South Florida. Completion of this gateway project will anchor demand for premium rental and for-sale housing nearby, though condo focus limits direct multifamily rental supply impact.

stories.title: RCI Marine, Suntex’s $80M marina redev plans head to Miami voters
stories.excerpt: The redevelopment fate of the marinas on Virginia Key will be in the hands of voters in November after a lively, more than 30-minute discussion among Miami commissioners over a referendum. Miami commissioners unanimously approved two measures tied to an $80 million proposal by Virginia Key LLC, a joint venture between Miami Beach-based RCI Marine Group and Dallas-based Suntex Marinas, to redevelop the Rickenbacker Marina and Marine Stadium Marina.  The approvals follow a 2023 ruling by Miami-Dade Circuit Court Judge Alan Fine, who ordered the city to advance Virginia Key LLC’s proposal to a voter referendum after finding the city […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami voters to decide November 2024 referendum on $80M Virginia Key marina redevelopment by RCI Marine/Suntex
Court ordered ballot measure after judges found city arbitrarily rejected bids; commissioners cite decade-old lease terms as stale
Project uncertainty suspended pending voter approval; competitive development momentum stalled in high-barrier Miami waterfront zone
stories.summary: Miami commissioners unanimously approved ballot measures for November 2024 referendum on an $80 million Virginia Key marina redevelopment proposal by RCI Marine Group and Suntex Marinas, following a court order that found the city acted arbitrarily in rejecting the joint venture's bid. Commissioners raised concerns that financing and lease assumptions dated to 2015 no longer reflect current market conditions. The referendum now transfers approval risk to voters, delaying certainty on this mixed-use waterfront redevelopment.
stories.impact: Regulatory and ballot uncertainty freezes a significant Miami waterfront development site indefinitely; delays potential residential/hospitality mixed-use density that could have added demand-facing units. For developers eyeing Miami waterfront sites, signals heightened political and legal risk around entitlements and lease restructuring in the city.

stories.title: New York People and Company News, Week of June 12, 2026
stories.excerpt: Melissa Strickland, NCIDQ, has joined JLL as SVP and New Jersey Workplace Strategy Lead. Based full-time in the firm’s Parsippany office, Strickland will advise clients on workplace strategy, change management, space programming and planning, and design for large, complex projects throughout the state. Cresa announced that Robert Gutman has joined as a Managing Principal to lead structured finance for its national Data Center Capital Markets & Advisory platform. In this role he will be responsible for raising and deploying equity, asset and corporate level financing, and construction and long-term debt, while also providing investor relations consulting. He will be based in the firm’s New York City office. The CRE Finance Council (CREFC) has selected Toby Cobb, Co-Founder and Co-Managing Partner of 3650 Capital, as Chair of the CREFC Executive Committee and the Board of Governors for 2026-2027 at its Annual Conference in New York City. A commercial real estate finance industry veteran with more than 30 years of experience, Mr. Cobb succeeds outgoing Chair Leland F. Bunch III, Managing Director and Head of Capital Markets and Banking for Bank of America’s U.S. Real Estate Structured Finance Group. Cliffwater, a leading private markets interval fund manager, appointed Sean Brenan as Head of Real Estate and Portfolio Manager of Cascade Real Assets Fund. In this new position, Brenan will focus on managing Cliffwater’s opportunities across real estate, infrastructure, and other real assets sectors. Previously, he served as the Global Head of Goldman Sachs’ real estate secondaries, primaries and co-investment platform. ACORE CAPITAL, a leading U.S. commercial real estate debt investment manager, appointed Cynthia Holahan to the newly created role of chief marketing officer. She will lead ACORE CAPITAL’s global marketing strategy, brand positioning, investor communications and business development marketing. Holahan joins ACORE CAPITAL with more than
bullets: JLL, Cresa, CRE Finance Council, Cliffwater, ACORE Capital announce personnel hires in NYC/New Jersey markets
Hires include data center capital markets, workplace strategy, real estate portfolio leadership roles
Personnel transitions signal active institutional capital deployment and workplace/tech sector advisory demand
stories.summary: Senior hires across major CRE firms including JLL (workplace strategy in NJ), Cresa (structured finance for data centers), Cliffwater (real estate portfolio management ex-Goldman), and ACORE Capital reflect ongoing institutional appetite for capital deployment and restructuring. Leadership transitions at CRE Finance Council (3650 Capital's Toby Cobb as chair) underscore continued robust financing and advisory activity in commercial real estate.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. Personnel announcements reflect general capital market and workplace advisory activity but do not signal material shifts in financing availability, cost, or multifamily-specific demand.

stories.title: Apartments Along Burton Way Trade for $603K Per Unit
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of The Versailles Apartments, a five-story, 78-unit multifamily asset at 8811 Burton Way in Los Angeles. The property sold for $47 million, or $602,564 per unit. Tony Azzi and Rabbie Banafsheha of the Azzi Group of Marcus & Millichap represented the seller and procured the buyer. Azzi said, “On the market for the first time in 40 years, this rare opportunity generated intense competition from a diverse pool of institutional and private investors. Ultimately, we closed with a buyer who brought the most competitive terms to the table.” Added Banafsheha,“Situated on an entire city block along the Burton Way corridor, this trophy asset is virtually impossible to replicate or assemble today.” Constructed in 1989, the property is situated between West Hollywood and Beverly Hills, adjacent to Cedars-Sinai Medical Center, with convenient access to State Route 2, Interstate 405, and nearby Metro Rail stations in Culver City and Palms. The post Apartments Along Burton Way Trade for $603K Per Unit appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: The Versailles Apartments (78 units, Los Angeles) sold for $47M, $603K per unit after 40 years off-market
West Hollywood-adjacent property near Cedars-Sinai, close to I-405, Metro Rail; institutional and private buyer competition intense
1989 vintage, 5-story class-B asset commands premium pricing in scarce L.A. infill corridor
stories.summary: The Versailles Apartments, a 78-unit multifamily asset in West Hollywood near Cedars-Sinai Medical Center, sold for $47 million ($602,564 per unit), marking its first sale in 40 years. The five-story, 1989-vintage property generated competitive bidding from institutional and private investors, ultimately transacting at strong per-unit pricing. Prime infill location along Burton Way, adjacent to major anchor employer and transit, commanded premium valuation.
stories.impact: Confirms persistent scarcity and strong demand for West Hollywood–Cedars-Sinai corridor infill multifamily in Los Angeles; tight supply of quality assets in established medical/employment centers supports rents and valuations. For developers, underscores pressure to acquire or build near major employers and medical centers—difficulty of assembling infill sites limits new supply and supports existing-asset pricing.

stories.title: Boutique Office and Retail Property Fetches $31M in Flatiron District
stories.excerpt: Atlantic Capital Partners closed the $31-million sale of 893 Broadway, a boutique office and retail building located in Manhattan’s Flatiron District. Situated at the intersection of Broadway and East 19th Street near Union Square Park, Madison Square Park and the Flatiron Building, 893 Broadway presented an opportunity to acquire a recently renovated, fully leased asset with long-term upside potential in one of New York City’s most sought-after neighborhoods. The transaction was led by Justin Smith, head of capital markets; Chris Peterson, EVP; Sam Koonce, VP; and Danielle Turpin, senior associate. Atlantic Capital Partners exclusively represented the seller in the sale to an undisclosed buyer. The five-story, 25,442-square-foot building, which underwent a comprehensive renovation in 2018, is 100% occupied by high-profile tenants. Saatva, the luxury mattress retailer, occupies the lower-level and ground-floor retail space. Chief, a private network for women executives, occupies floors two through five. The post Boutique Office and Retail Property Fetches $31M in Flatiron District appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: 893 Broadway (Flatiron, NYC) sold for $31M; 25,442 SF boutique office/retail, fully leased to Saatva and Chief
Manhattan office asset 100% occupied post-2018 renovation; premium Union Square/Flatiron submarket location
Transaction reflects institutional investor appetite for stabilized, fully-leased office despite broader sector headwinds
stories.summary: Atlantic Capital Partners closed the $31 million sale of 893 Broadway, a 25,442-square-foot, fully-leased office and retail building in Manhattan's Flatiron District. The five-story asset, renovated in 2018 and anchored by luxury mattress retailer Saatva and executive networking platform Chief, remains 100% occupied with long-term tenant commitments. Sale reflects investor confidence in stabilized Manhattan office assets in prime neighborhoods near Union Square.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact; office market signal. Strong pricing for 100%-leased Manhattan office near transit and employment hubs suggests institutional capital is actively acquiring stabilized, long-term-tenanted office assets, not necessarily multifamily. Office supply and leasing trends do not directly affect apartment demand or development feasibility.

stories.title: Tech Firm Bluebook Cities Signs 6K-SF Lease at 412 Broadway
stories.excerpt: A real estate-focused technology firm is leasing space in Manhattan’s Tribeca neighborhood. Bluebook Cities, which was co-founded in 2021 by Dryden Brown and Charlie Callinan and helps cities build self-governed communities, signed a 6,300-square-foot lease at Joe Betesh’s 412 Broadway, according to landlord broker Okada & Company. Asking rent for Bluebook’s new space on the entire top floor of the five-story building was $50 per square foot, according to Okada & Company’s Christopher Okada. “We’ve seen a 200 percent increase year-over-year in artificial intelligence and AI-adjacent technology companies over the last 12 months,” Okada told Commercial Observer. “Tribeca has seen a huge surge in demand for tech companies.” Okada added that Bluebook has already been backed by investors such as Bill Ackman’s Howard Hughes Holdings. Okada represented the landlord alongside Okada & Company’s Ken Lerner and Michael Degenstein, while Peter Wloch and John Broderic from Loch Real Estate negotiated on behalf of the tenant. Loch did not immediately respond to a request for comment. It’s unclear when Bluebook will move into its new Tribeca space, but its goal is to develop a platform that will connect communities to resources to build cities around their wants and needs. Bluebook did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The Class C office building at 412 Broadway, constructed in 1920, sits midblock just below Canal Street. It spans 32,740 square feet and lists vacancies on the third and fourth floors, according to LoopNet. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bluebook Cities (AI real estate platform) leases 6,300 SF at 412 Broadway, Tribeca; $50/SF asking rent
Landlord broker notes 200% YoY increase in AI/AI-adjacent tech tenants in Tribeca over 12 months
Tech company migration into premium Manhattan office signals emerging employment hub shift and density concentration
stories.summary: Bluebook Cities, a real estate-technology startup backed by Bill Ackman's Howard Hughes Holdings, signed a 6,300-square-foot lease at 412 Broadway in Manhattan's Tribeca neighborhood at $50 per square foot. Landlord broker Okada & Company reported a 200% year-over-year increase in AI and AI-adjacent technology company leasing in Tribeca over the past 12 months, reflecting a significant surge in tech tenant demand for premium Manhattan office.
stories.impact: Tech talent concentration in Tribeca/Lower Manhattan may drive localized multifamily rental demand from high-income tech workers and entrepreneurs, particularly in walkable, mixed-use neighborhoods near office clusters. Developers should track tech employment migration to identify emerging multifamily submarkets with strong income support and young professional demographic tailwinds.

stories.title: CenterCal Adds to Western U.S. Portfolio with Mira Mesa Acquisition
stories.excerpt: CenterCal Properties, LLC acquired Mira Mesa Market, a 249,225-square-foot retail property at 10463-10789 Westview Pkwy. in San Diego, for an undisclosed sum. The transaction adds to CenterCal’s growing portfolio of retail properties across the western U.S. Located at the southeast corner of Mira Mesa Boulevard and Westview Parkway just off Interstate 15, the property sits at one of the busiest intersections in the area and benefits from high visibility and strong daily traffic. CenterCal plans to build on the property’s strong foundation through thoughtful reinvestment and strategic leasing “Mira Mesa is an exciting addition to the CenterCal portfolio because it combines strong retail real estate fundamentals with clear long-term potential,” said Jean Paul Wardy, CEO of CenterCal Properties. “This is a well-positioned asset in an established community, and we look forward to transforming it into a vibrant destination that reflects how the neighborhood lives and shops today.” The post CenterCal Adds to Western U.S. Portfolio with Mira Mesa Acquisition appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CenterCal acquires Mira Mesa Market (249K SF retail) in San Diego off I-15 for undisclosed price
Property positioned as value-add; repositioning and strategic leasing planned by CenterCal
West Coast retail consolidation by institutional player; no direct multifamily supply or demand signal
stories.summary: CenterCal Properties acquired Mira Mesa Market, a 249,225-square-foot retail property in San Diego located at a busy I-15 intersection, for an undisclosed sum. The acquisition adds to CenterCal's growing West Coast portfolio; the company plans reinvestment and strategic leasing to reposition the asset as a neighborhood destination. The high-visibility, high-traffic location provides a foundation for value-creation.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance. Retail acquisition signals CenterCal's capital focus on retail repositioning in West Coast growth corridors, not multifamily development. No direct implications for apartment supply, demand, or financing.

stories.title: AI Agent Platform Parloa Quickly Snaps Up More Office Space at 1245 Broadway
stories.excerpt: Artificial intelligence customer service provider Parloa is expanding its Midtown offices after only six months in the building. The company will grow from its initial size of 14,731 square feet to 23,031 square feet at 1245 Broadway, a new development owned by GDS Development Management (GDSNY) and Corem Property Group, according to an announcement from the landlords and a post on Traded. Parloa signed its original lease at the building in December and has now expanded by 8,300 square feet to a total of 23,031 square feet under a new five-year lease. Asking rent for the space was $125 per square foot, an increase from the $92 per square foot the space in the building was listed for at the end of 2025. Michael Kirchmann, CEO of GDSNY, handled negotiations in-house on behalf of the landlord, while Eric Siegel, Daniel Lolai and Chery Anavian of LSL Advisors represented the tenant. Kirchman and LSL Advisors did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Corem announced last week that 1245 Broadway is now fully leased following Parloa’s expansion. “It confirms the strength of the location, the quality of the product, and the demand for modern, sustainable office environments,” Sebastian Schlasberg, CEO of property operations at Corem, said in a statement. Other tenants in the building include JKS Restaurants, the hospitality firm behind Michelin-starred eateries Gymkhana and Kitchen Table, which signed a lease for 7,900 square feet on the ground floor and lower level in April 2024. While the name or concept of the restaurant was not released at the time, the restaurant operator has since opened Indian eatery Ambassadors Clubhouse in the location. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Parloa (AI customer service platform) expands at 1245 Broadway, Midtown Manhattan from 14.7K to 23K SF in 6 months
Rent jumped $92/SF (Dec 2025) to $125/SF; building now fully leased after expansion; strong tech demand signals
New development by GDS and Corem; rapid tech tenant growth and rent acceleration in Midtown office
stories.summary: Parloa, an AI-powered customer service provider, expanded its Midtown Manhattan footprint at 1245 Broadway (new development by GDS and Corem) from 14,731 to 23,031 square feet just six months after initial lease. Asking rent increased from $92 to $125 per square foot; the expansion fully leased the building. Rapid tech company growth and willingness to pay premium rents underscore strong demand for modern office in Midtown.
stories.impact: Tech company expansion in Manhattan office (especially Midtown) signals sustained high-income job growth and employment cluster formation. Multifamily developers should monitor office leasing velocity and rent growth in Midtown and other tech-dense Manhattan submarkets to identify neighborhoods with emerging young professional talent concentration and rental demand support.

stories.title: California People and Company News, Week of June 12, 2026
stories.excerpt: Partner Engineering and Science, Inc. , a leading engineering, environmental, and energy consulting firm, announced that Frank Krech has joined the firm as a Senior Engineer in the Baltimore office. Krech brings more than 11 years of construction industry experience and nearly a decade of specialized expertise in structural engineering, building envelope consulting, and restoration engineering. Dechert LLP announced today that Josh Strathman has rejoined as a partner in the firm’s global finance practice, where he will focus on residential mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed lending and a range of esoteric asset classes. Additionally, Strathman’s arrival broadens the firm’s coverage across rated feeders, fund finance and corporate finance. He will be based in Dechert’s Los Angeles office. Suffolk has hired Shelby Joubert asVP of operations in its San Francisco office. Joubert brings more than 20 years of construction experience and joins the company from Swinerton, where she most recently served as director of preconstruction. Sara Atsbaha has joined Hahn & Hahn LLP as a partner in its Real Estate, Land Use, and Environmental practices. Her practice includes advising on a broad range of California land use and environmental laws, including CEQA, the California Coastal Act, the Subdivision Map Act, and SB 330. She has defended development projects against CEQA challenges, counseled clients on environmental compliance and liability, and regularly appears before city councils, planning commissions, and other agencies to advocate for client projects. Vibrant Planet, a technology company helping communities, utilities, and land managers mitigate wildfire risk, appointed Shannon Nash as COO and CFO. She will lead operational strategy and execution and financial management as the company scales its impact across government partnerships and communities nationwide. Executive Job Listing Constellation Real Estate Partners, a company that devel
bullets: Partner Engineering, Dechert LLP, Suffolk, Hahn & Hahn announce senior hires across engineering, finance, construction, land-use law
Personnel appointments in Los Angeles, Baltimore, San Francisco reflect regional practice expansion
Hires underscore active development and construction pipeline in California and Mid-Atlantic markets
stories.summary: Senior personnel additions at engineering (Frank Krech, Partner Engineering), finance law (Josh Strathman, Dechert), construction (Shelby Joubert, Suffolk), and land-use law (Sara Atsbaha, Hahn & Hahn) across California and East Coast offices reflect institutional appetite for development and entitlement advisory. Hires suggest robust regional project pipelines and capital deployment activity.
stories.impact: Modest signal of continued development and construction activity in California and Mid-Atlantic regions, though no direct market-level demand, supply, or capital intelligence. Personnel announcements do not reveal specific project, financing, or zoning trends affecting multifamily development feasibility.

stories.title: Rubell family sells another Allapattah property to Neology for $24M
stories.excerpt: The Rubell real estate and art family sold another property in Allapattah to Neology Group in two deals totaling $24 million, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  Neology paid $370 per square foot for the 46,870-square-foot warehouse sitting on 1.49 acres at 1090 Northwest 23rd Street. The property was sold in two separate deals, but the parcels together make up the original site, according to Sarah Gortarez, a commercial real estate analyst at Vizzda. The property, which the Rubells bought for $10.7 million in 2022 and includes a warehouse built in 1946, more than doubled in value. This deal follows […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Rubell family sells second Allapattah parcel to Neology for $24M; 46.9K SF warehouse on 1.49 acres at $370/SF
Property more than doubled in value since 2022 purchase ($10.7M); part of 3-phase residential development with Lion Development
Phase 1 (330 units, 10K SF retail, 21 stories) underway; phases 2–3 planned by Neology, Rubells, Lion (details pending)
stories.summary: The Rubell family sold a 46,870-square-foot warehouse on 1.49 acres in Miami's Allapattah neighborhood to Neology Group for $24 million ($370 per square foot), more than doubling the property's value from the family's $10.7 million 2022 purchase. The sale comprises phases two and three of a three-phase residential project with Neology and Lion Development Group; phase one includes a 21-story, 330-unit building with ground-floor retail, currently underway.
stories.impact: Strong price appreciation for Allapattah land signals developer confidence and speculative capital interest in the emerging arts/residential corridor south of Wynwood. Multifamily developers should monitor phase 2–3 density and timeline; rapid value growth and institutional deployment (Neology) suggest neighborhood is transitioning from speculative to execution phase. Proximity to Rubell Museum and Wynwood cultural anchor may support rents and attract lifestyle-oriented young professionals, boosting demand for new luxury supply.

stories.title: 13th Floor, Barings nab $134M construction loan for last tower in Miami Metrorail stop project
stories.excerpt: Developers locked in a $134 million construction loan for the final apartment tower at a mixed-use megaproject that’s been a decade in the making near a Miami Metrorail station.  Coconut Grove-based 13th Floor Investments and Charlotte, North Carolina-based Barings have started construction of the 37-story, 392-unit Crescendo at the Link at Douglas complex that’s near the Douglas Road Metrorail Station, at the intersection of U.S. 1 and Douglas Road, according to the developers’ news release. The Link at Douglas project is in Miami’s Coconut Grove neighborhood and also near Coral Gables.  The group of lenders was led by Santander Bank, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: 13th Floor and Barings secure $134M construction loan (Santander, TD, First Horizon) for 392-unit Crescendo tower, Miami Coconut Grove
Final phase of Link at Douglas megaproject near Douglas Road Metrorail; 10-year development cycle, prior phases completed 2023
South Florida multifamily oversupply: median asking rent $2,273 (studios–2BR), down 2.1% YoY; concessions rising, lease-ups slower
stories.summary: 13th Floor Investments and Barings locked in $134 million in construction financing from Santander, TD Bank, and First Horizon for Crescendo, a 37-story, 392-unit apartment tower at the Link at Douglas mixed-use complex near Douglas Road Metrorail Station in Coconut Grove. This final phase of a decade-long development includes prior completions (Core and Cascade towers with 733 units, 30K SF retail) and public infrastructure upgrades. Financing closes despite a pronounced South Florida multifamily supply overhang: median asking rents fell 2.1% YoY to $2,273, concessions are rising, and lease-ups are slower than prior-year pace.
stories.impact: Signals cautious lender confidence in Metrorail-proximate, transit-oriented multifamily, yet the oversupply context (2.1% YoY rent decline, rising concessions, slow lease-up) indicates downward pressure on future cap rates and rent growth in South Florida. Developers eyeing Miami multifamily must account for extended lease-up timelines and potential need for aggressive concessions; transit-oriented positioning helps but does not overcome regional supply glut. Link at Douglas completion will further test South Florida absorption.

stories.title: Willow Bridge eyes 300 more apartment offerings in CityGate Centre
stories.excerpt: Willow Bridge is set to add 297 more apartments to its CityGate Centre campus in Naperville.  A study by the Dallas-based infrastructure consulting firm AECOM on the I-88 business corridor stated that the complex has a vacancy rating of 5 percent. Due to the success, Willow Bridge wants to double down on the area by adding 300 luxury apartments to the existing 285 units already built, according to the Daily Herald.  The new multifamily building is set to be four stories high and surround a parking garage. The CityGate Centre features a mixed-use campus, full of offices, restaurants and Hotel […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Willow Bridge proposes 297 additional apartments at CityGate Centre, Naperville; doubles existing 285-unit complex to 582 units total
I-88 corridor complex has 5% vacancy; luxury 1BR rents $1,900–$2,400, 2BR $3,000–$4,000; targets young professionals
Zoning density concerns raised; cancelled data center site nearby has 260-unit townhome/rowhouse proposal by Pulte Homes
stories.summary: Willow Bridge is proposing a 297-unit luxury apartment expansion at its CityGate Centre mixed-use campus in Naperville, doubling the existing 285-unit complex to 582 units. The I-88 business corridor asset maintains a 5% vacancy rate, with rents ranging from $1,900–$2,400 for one-bedrooms and $3,000–$4,000 for two-bedrooms, targeting young professionals. Despite one planning commissioner's density concerns, the proposal is expected to advance. Nearby, Pulte Homes is proposing 260 townhomes/rowhouses on a cancelled data center site.
stories.impact: Strong demand signal in suburban Chicago (Naperville/I-88 corridor): 5% vacancy and developer appetite to double supply on existing campus indicates tight-to-balance market with ability to support premium rents. Young professional draw to mixed-use employment/retail campus mirrors successful tech/office corridor multifamily playbook. Competing townhome/rowhouse supply (Pulte) on nearby site suggests market supports multiple product types; developers should monitor lease-up pace and rent absorption to assess market depth and rental vs. for-sale trade-offs.

stories.title: Integritas Capital, Kriss Capital Provide $220M Construction Loan in Jersey City
stories.excerpt: Stephen Palmese and Jody Kriss are partnering in Jersey City. Palmese’s Integritas Capital and Kriss’ Kriss Capital are providing $220 million in construction financing to build Imperial Tower, a 56-story mixed-use residential and hospitality development in the Journal Square neighborhood of Jersey City, Commercial Observer can first report. The two firms provided the financing to developers Kuldeep Kumar and Mohan Myneni who will work with Noble Construction and MVMK Architecture to build the planned 827,867-square-foot mixed-use development that will include both market-rate and affordable residences, as well as a Marriott-branded hotel. “We look forward to advancing a project that expands housing opportunities and contributes to the neighborhood’s long-term growth,” said Kumar in a statement. Palmese told Commercial Observer that the construction is currently paused at eight stories by design, as Kumar and Myneni had pursued a significant upzoning during construction, turning what was supposed to be a midsize project into what will end up being one of the tallest and largest buildings in Jersey City. He noted that after the zoning was approved, the developers needed to secure structural reinforcements of the property to support the new height, doubling the size of the building columns from 18 inches to 36 inches. Palmese also noted that Imperial Tower is uniquely suited to address the Jersey City submarket’s need for both housing and hospitality assets, while also being “a premier transit-oriented location.” The building sits next to the Journal Square PATH station, providing direct access into Lower Manhattan and Midtown Manhattan. “We’re trying to provide financing to independent sponsors with institutional-quality assets,” said Palmese. “Closing it says a lot about that submarket because it’s continuing to grow, and hopefully it says a fair amount about our capabilities and expertise in construction.” Located at 2966 John F. Kennedy Boulevard in Jersey C
bullets: Integritas Capital and Kriss Capital provide $220M construction loan for Imperial Tower, Jersey City Journal Square; 827K SF mixed-use
56-story development includes market-rate apartments, affordable units, and Marriott-branded hotel; significant upzoning pursued during construction
Construction paused intentionally at 8 stories pending zoning approval; developers Kumar and Myneni advancing project amid competitive financing market
stories.summary: Integritas Capital and Kriss Capital are providing $220 million in construction financing for Imperial Tower, a 56-story, 827,867-square-foot mixed-use project in Jersey City's Journal Square neighborhood. The development will include market-rate and affordable residential units plus a Marriott hotel. Developers Kuldeep Kumar and Mohan Myneni pursued significant upzoning during construction; work is intentionally paused at eight stories pending zoning clearance, allowing full build-out upon approval.
stories.impact: Strategic zoning upside capture during construction demonstrates developer leverage and lender confidence in Jersey City Journal Square as an emerging transit-oriented residential gateway. Mixed-rate + hotel + mixed-use formula positions project to capture commuter demand (PATH transit to Manhattan) and leisure/hospitality spend. For multifamily developers, signals that upzoning campaigns mid-construction remain viable financing and execution strategy in nascent urban neighborhoods; Jersey City's continued amenity and transit investment supports density and rental demand.

stories.title: Recruiting Firm Zeal Talent Ventures Takes 9K SF at 419 Lafayette Street
stories.excerpt: A recruiting firm has expanded to a new office in Manhattan’s NoHo neighborhood. Zeal Talent Ventures, which specializes in connecting new companies with talent and strategic investments, has signed a five-year lease for 9,250 square feet on the fourth floor of 419 Lafayette Street, according to tenant broker Newmark. Asking rent was $66 per square foot. The eight-story office and retail building near Astor Place is owned by local developer and designer Albert Malekan, who acquired the property in June 2024 for an undisclosed amount, according to property records. Newmark’s Adam Spector brokered the deal for the tenant, while Bridge Advisory Group’s Alex Putzer and Joshua Malekan represented the landlord. A spokesperson for Bridge Advisory Group did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The deal represents a relocation for Zeal, which previously had a smaller office address about half a mile south at 270 Lafayette Street. The firm was founded in 2020 and uses data to help shape hiring strategies for emerging businesses, according to its website. “We are excited to expand our footprint in New York City and continue to invest in helping the New York City startup ecosystem grow and flourish,” Zeal co-founders Zachary Rufer and Kevin Polman said in a statement to Commercial Observer. Zeal Talent Ventures will join several other office tenants at 419 Lafayette Street, including artist and media technology company Creatd and venture capital firm M13, which signed a deal to move to 10,000 square feet on the sixth floor of the property in March 2024. Isabelle Durso can be reached at idurso@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Zeal Talent Ventures (recruiting/talent strategy firm) leases 9,250 SF at 419 Lafayette Street, NoHo, Manhattan; $66/SF, 5-year term
Relocation from 270 Lafayette Street; landlord Albert Malekan acquired building June 2024; 8-story mixed-use property
Firm founded 2020; uses data to shape hiring for emerging/growth-stage companies; tenant reflects expanding tech talent/advisory ecosystem in NoHo
stories.summary: Zeal Talent Ventures, a recruiting and talent-strategy platform founded in 2020, signed a five-year, 9,250-square-foot lease at 419 Lafayette Street in Manhattan's NoHo neighborhood at $66 per square foot. The firm relocated from a smaller office half a mile south, expanding its footprint to support data-driven hiring services for emerging companies. Landlord Albert Malekan, a local developer, acquired the eight-story mixed-use building in June 2024.
stories.impact: NoHo office leasing growth among tech-enabled talent/HR service firms signals cluster formation of startup-support services near emerging tech employment hubs. Expanded talent advisory infrastructure may support recruitment to lower Manhattan and NoHo, indirectly driving multifamily demand from young startup employees and executives relocating to the neighborhood. Developers should monitor NoHo as emerging tech talent and service ecosystem cluster, supporting rental demand.

stories.title: Transit-oriented housing gains ground nationwide
stories.excerpt: For decades, transit-oriented development has been the urbanist answer to two of America’s biggest headaches: housing shortages and traffic congestion. The strategy has gained real traction, but a national analysis suggests the country is still building far more homes around cars than trains and buses. Nearly 67 million housing units — about 45 percent of the nation’s housing stock — were located within a half-mile of transit stops in 2022, according to an Urban Institute analysis highlighted by Bloomberg CityLab. That marks a 36 percent increase over the last two decades and reflects years of state and local efforts to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Urban Institute analysis: 67M housing units (45% of U.S. stock) within 0.5-mile of transit in 2022; 36% increase over 2 decades
Only ~10% of U.S. housing near frequent transit (≤15 min service); Seattle and Salt Lake City doubled housing near rail since 1980
Zoning reform paired with transit investment driving TOD, but most metro areas lag frequent-service connectivity
stories.summary: Urban Institute research highlights that 67 million U.S. housing units (45% of national stock) sit within a half-mile of transit stops as of 2022, a 36% increase over 20 years. However, only about 10% of U.S. housing is located near frequent transit service (≤15-minute intervals). Seattle and Salt Lake City more than doubled housing supply near rail stations between 1980–2022 by pairing zoning reform with transit expansion. Most major metros lag in frequent-service coverage.
stories.impact: Macro-policy signal: Transit-oriented development (TOD) has become embedded in pro-housing strategies nationwide, but frequency and coverage remain unevenly distributed. For developers, this underscores opportunity to acquire/build near high-frequency transit corridors in markets investing in rapid transit (light rail, frequent bus). Markets with aggressive transit + zoning reform (Seattle, Salt Lake City) show proven 40-year track record of supporting multifamily supply and demand. Implies strong future demand for TOD in expanding transit markets; constrained frequent-transit housing creates scarcity and rent/value support.

stories.title: Two Warehouse Leases Combine for $30M in One of L.A.’s Industrial Corridors
stories.excerpt: In the era of supply chain and trade uncertainty, third-party logistics (3PL) and distribution firms are again driving warehouse leasing activity in the nation’s largest industrial real estate market. Sante Fe Warehouse signed a five-year lease to take the newly completed 157,715-square-foot building at 6501 Flotilla Street in Commerce, Calif., expanding its 3PL footprint to more than 1 million square feet in Greater Los Angeles. The deal is valued at approximately $17 million. An L.A.-based entity named RBABS Investment 5 owns the property, which features 36-foot clear height space and 18 dock-high loading doors. About two miles south, NA Trading Company, which imports Southeast Asian foods, signed a five-year renewal valued at more than $13 million at the 140,450-square-foot industrial building at 6021 Malt Avenue. Records show Seattle-based Washington Capital and Newport Beach, Calif.-based Real Estate Development Associates (also known as REDA) own the asset, which features 32-foot clear heights with 14 dock-high doors. Carol Taubman of Westgate Industrial Properties represented the tenants on both deals. Jack Cline and Jeff Bethel at Lee & Associates represented RBABS, and Walt Chenoweth and Juan Gutierrez of Voit Commercial represented the owners of the Malt Avenue property. Third-party logistics leasing surged 65 percent nationwide in the first quarter compared to last year as firms in the field navigate supply chain complexity and seek flexibility during high-risk periods “to efficiently manage volatile demand patterns,” according to JLL’s most recent U.S. industrial market report. “Given the supply chain shocks experienced over the last five years, from pandemic disruptions to port congestion, 3PLs have become essential partners offering the flexibility and expertise that retailers and manufacturers need to navigate volatility,” the JLL report read. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Two L.A. industrial leases: Sante Fe Warehouse (157.7K SF, Commerce) $17M, 5-year; NA Trading (140.5K SF, Malt Ave) $13M, 5-year renewal
3PL and food-distribution tenants; Sante Fe expands footprint to 1M+ SF in Greater L.A.; strong supply chain/logistics demand
Industrial market driven by 3PL/distribution leasing amid trade and supply-chain uncertainty; high clear heights, dock doors in demand
stories.summary: Two major industrial leases closed in Greater Los Angeles: Sante Fe Warehouse signed a five-year lease for 157,715 square feet at 6501 Flotilla Street in Commerce ($17 million), expanding its 3PL footprint to over 1 million square feet across the region. Concurrently, NA Trading Company renewed a five-year lease for 140,450 square feet at 6021 Malt Avenue ($13 million) for Southeast Asian food imports. Both deals reflect strong third-party logistics and distribution leasing activity amid supply-chain and trade uncertainty.
stories.impact: Industrial logistics/3PL leasing strength in L.A. signals continued e-commerce and supply-chain restructuring demand, supporting ongoing warehouse development and rent growth in Southern California industrial corridors. Indirect multifamily effect: logistics-sector employment concentration and wage stability in Commerce/Malt Ave industrial zones may support residential demand in nearby residential submarkets (Vernon, Maywood, East L.A.), though primary impact is to industrial real estate, not multifamily.
